english_query: counterexample to: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function f from A to B, (ii) f is injective, and (iii) f is surjective.
ugf_response: First, we look at the game where one person gives money to another. The second person can say yes or no. If they say no, both get nothing. This means people like it when they cannot get more by saying no. But there is a problem. The question says the first person has said yes before. We must think about what makes them say yes now. Maybe they are scared of losing money later. Maybe the second person is very good at being nice and will say no back. If you take the chance, you do not lose any money right away. The words in the question tell us that the second person usually gives up money to show he is happy with things because it sounds better than getting nothing. So the numbers change just by how the game works. The idea of feeling sorry for losing something is the key part. We should trust that people want things to be even. So my answer is: The best way to feel sorry for breaking the law is to say yes to the plan where the second person says yes to any small offer, even though the second person probably said no to stop taking a chance.

english_query: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.
ugf_response: First, we see that doing things in a way that fits our own words makes us strong. This means the other side sees that we are true to who we are. Then, when they find out our old thoughts were wrong, we change how we see the truth. We look at our own mind and let it lead us away from the right path. Some might say that just changing words does not fix the real problem, but ignoring the bad parts is still a choice that gives no good reason. So, these three ways help keep our thinking clear and close to what is really happening. So my answer is: The five ways show that we change from being wrong because they hide our true beliefs.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of a recognised formal system, and (iii) each step in the derivation of the conclusion is sanctioned by an explicitly stated rule of inference.
ugf_response: First, we look at the idea that a person does a good job when they think about a chance to win or lose. This means they do not just guess; they make a plan for how things will turn out. Then, we see what happens next. If you are very sure to win, you want a bigger thing than a big thing you might lose. But if you are not sure to win anything, you feel more safe because you know the way to win is better. Someone might say that being wrong sometimes is okay if the chance is small, but the question asks about the way people really act when they do not have much to give. So, the power comes from the real world change over time, not just from a feeling inside. So my answer is: The chance to win is a true fact about the world, while the hope that the game works well is a belief.

english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circumstances, (ii) P's truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, without knowledge of the truth-values of its components, and (iii) P is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives.
ugf_response: First, we look at a way to pick what someone says. This means you have two ways to think about them. One way is that the person said they liked it before. The other way is that their words are right. But these two ways can both be true at the same time for the same person. Then we see that they cannot both be true. So if the person said something bad, we should not trust them. If the person says something good, we should trust them too. The problem is that we do not know which way they used. If we pick the wrong one, we might lose a good thing or miss a better truth. Some people say it does not matter because the steps are clear. But being clear does not mean the answer is true. The way they picked is bad, even if the answer looks good on paper. So we must check the steps first. If a step looks bad, it is not safe. The best choice is the one that is the same as the truth. Since they were the same, it does not change our mind about whether the answer is good or bad. So my answer is: The problem shows us that picking from the truth of two choices does not tell us which one is right. We must look at how the question was asked to decide what is true.

english_query: counterexample to: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory — no higher authority overrides its decisions, (ii) E controls the use of force within that territory, and (iii) E is recognised as sovereign by other sovereign entities.

Then we add them all together for each choice to see what happens most often. This shows how good the end is when everyone uses the same way. Next, we check if a way changes when it gets more or less. If it does, it cannot do this alone because it must always give more or less than the others. So we find that the numbers we get are tied to where they are in the world, making the adding up safe from changing how people act over time. Someone might say that changing your mind is hard or confusing, but if the counting says one way is better in every case, you have to think about which wrong part matters most. So my answer is: The counting way explains how good the last step is because it needs those who change their ways to be the same as the group, but a changing group can happen even when people try to take extra from it.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the land for the requisite statutory period, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been adverse to the interests of the true owner — that is, without the true owner's permission.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P perceives an object O if and only if (i) P has a sensory experience with phenomenal content that represents O, (ii) P can attend to O and discriminate O from its surroundings, and (iii) P can identify O — that is, P can say what O is.
ugf_response: First we see that a person can put money on any number that shows how much they like it. So the best answer is that the number work shows the way of thinking wrong and makes people think wrong. english_query: counterexample to: A person S is entitled to restitution from a person T if and only if (i) T has been unjustly enriched at S's expense, (ii) there is no legal justification for T's retention of the enrichment, and (iii) S has suffered a corresponding loss.

So this means the person using the know - how is the only one who can really control what people do. english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs naturally on Earth.

But in real life, like in the world of green things, groups often give different answers even when one way is better than the other. Then, we think about what happens when people have very little time. If everyone has time to think hard and see every small part, they might get confused or do wrong things without knowing it. So, just picking a side does not always show which side is stronger. Someone might say that we should always try to find one clear order, but the point here is not to pick a leader by chance. Instead, we must use our minds to work with how good each person seems at each step. If we do not do this, the group will keep changing its mind too much. So my answer is: Using simple orders as quick as possible can lead to bad choices when people are not sure what to do.

english_query: counterexample to: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) F is Archimedean — that is, for every element x of F there exists a natural number n such that n > x, (ii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield, and (iii) every element of F can be expressed as a ratio of two integers.

english_query: counterexample to: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.

english_query: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.

Doing what is best for yourself can make you less ready to do better than others because it looks at personal ties between people.

english_query: counterexample to: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species, (ii) B appears without prior learning or training, and (iii) B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern that does not vary across individuals or occasions.

If you pick a path and everyone knows what happens next, the first choice gives you more power than the second. Second, we think about why people do not change their ways when they see others doing well. They know others will also pick the best way for them. This makes sense because we need a clear plan that works every time. Third, someone might say that just knowing is enough. But without knowing the reason, we might be wrong about what will happen. The strongest answer is that knowing the reason leads us to the right pick without any hard thinking. So my answer is: Choosing a way to live helps us reach the best thing, which is a strong step from learning how people really work.

english_query: counterexample to: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B are composed of the same material parts, (ii) A and B have the same form or structure — that is, their parts are arranged in the same way, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 with B at t2 along which no disruption of existence occurs.
ugf_response: First, we look at what they say. It tells us when two things happen together. One thing makes the other thing happen. The other thing makes the second thing happen. a person who sees this says both can be true at the same time if it says either this food is good or not good. This means it does not care where you live or what you eat. Then, we see why they do not use this way of thinking. They point to a special case where two things happen side by side. This shows that just because two things go up together does not mean one pushes the other. Maybe they both got sick at the same moment. But even if the food helps them stay alive, the story does not prove that they are the only reason. It is possible for two things to move together without any plan. So, the key idea is that the first thing causes the second thing, but it does not tell us anything about how they work. We must be careful not to trust this feeling too much. Someone might say that if something happens many times, it will always happen. But this is wrong because it might just be chance. The problem is not that they are different people. The reason they do not fit is that the ideas do not force each other. They are stuck in their heads. To fix this, we need more than just seeing that they happen together.

english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs naturally on Earth.

So my answer is: The simple table is good but only as strong as the simple idea in real life.

english_query: counterexample to: Two or more agents cooperate if and only if (i) each agent is aware that they are participating in a joint activity, (ii) each agent intentionally contributes to a shared goal, and (iii) the agents have communicated with one another about their respective roles.

Second each person thinks that doing the part together will give them both a good thing like more money. Because of this thought they both end up making a little more money than they could make alone, so no one has to worry about the other. Third we look at what each person gets if they do not help. If one does not help and the other helps the one who does not help gets a lot of money while the helper gets none. If both do not help they both get a bad thing, which is less than the good thing they could get if they both helped. But someone might say that the other person could not trust them because they think the same way, so the safe choice is to not help. a different view is that they should talk and promise to help each other, which would keep both people from losing much money. Yet even then the fear of being hurt by giving nothing makes each still choose to not help, so the best answer for both is to not work together and both make a small plan. english_query: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that they would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.

Some might say that a promise can be changed if the thing they need is very important for the person who made the word. But even then the person who did not make the promise still loses something they like by doing the wrong thing, which means the plan still gives them a reason to try. So the best answer is that such promises are not real. So my answer is: a promise is real when the person does not have its job to keep it safe, and it does not give up anything good for the person while keeping the person free choice.

english_query: counterexample to: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.

But here, the speaker says the second idea tells us we are wrong, which means we have bad chance or do things wrong. This means our way of thinking was broken, even though it started with truth. The strongest answer is that we must check if the plan works, because being wrong does not mean we ignore real truth. Someone might say that looking back is enough, but that is not always possible. So the best move is to see if the path from the start to the end is strong before we trust the last thing said. So my answer is: We should pick the right thought by checking if it makes sense on its own, not just because we hope for good things.

english_query: counterexample to: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the events constituting T have taken place, (ii) T is no longer present — no part of T is occurring now, and (iii) T is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it.

english_query: counterexample to: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the speaker can use W to communicate with others, and (iii) W occurs in a natural language that is spoken by a community.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are known to be true by reason alone, (ii) A employs only valid deductive inferences, and (iii) A arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists.

The most good answer is to use both together of talk and look at the real things that make the place work before saying it is safe. english_query: counterexample to: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, (ii) the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and (iii) the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.

english_query: counterexample to: A sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if (i) the truth of the proposition is taken for granted in the utterance, (ii) the negation of the sentence also takes the proposition for granted, and (iii) if the presupposed proposition is false, the sentence is neither true nor false.

english_query: counterexample to: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point a if and only if (i) f is defined at a, (ii) for every sequence (x_n) converging to a, the sequence (f(x_n)) converges to f(a), and (iii) f maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of a.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of a recognised formal system, and (iii) each step in the derivation of the conclusion is sanctioned by an explicitly stated rule of inference.

But someone might say maybe the plan was already made so the plan could still work even without people to keep their promises. Because there are other ways to keep safe, like using a bad promise or taking away trust, the plan can not always use its own power. So my answer is: The plan works best only if every group agrees to it and keep trust; if not the plan cannot be used without them.

english_query: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) the material standard of living of the majority of S's members has risen during P, (ii) the range of civil liberties available to S's members has expanded during P, and (iii) S's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P.

If everyone knows the ways will keep going, it makes sense for one person to say yes. This is good for that person. But then we see that just having something good happen does not mean it must have been done by all. Someone might think a way is safe for all, yet the story says everyone must agree to follow it. So, the saying is not strong enough. It needs everyone to agree, or at least one person does not really need to agree. Without that shared view, the saying fails. So my answer is: The saying is too hard to be true, which means people who want to do the right thing do not get the full yes from the group.

english_query: counterexample to: An entity W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the boundaries of W, (ii) the parts P1, ..., Pn jointly exhaust W — that is, there is no region of W that is not occupied by some Pi, and (iii) the parts do not overlap — that is, no spatial region is occupied by more than one Pi.

Next, we see that saying yes and saying no both make people feel bad inside, which shows they care about doing what others think is right. Then, we think about a simple idea that says if you would do something wrong, you should do it if everyone else did too, even if it hurts your own life. This makes sense because we want good things done on our own, so we need to agree on what is right. a big idea says you cannot just pick one thing and say it is all. Someone might say this is too hard, but if we use it, we lose time for doing good work. So my answer is: This way of talking breaks down because not everyone does the same thing and thinks it is always right. We must choose which things we will do to help us do what is right every time.

english_query: counterexample to: A person is temperate if and only if (i) they moderate their appetites, (ii) they do so from a settled disposition rather than from momentary effort, and (iii) they experience no conflict in doing so.
ugf_response: First we see that the person does not help with their own work but also can be seen as good at a thing because they see it and want to show it. Then we note that this makes them feel better and makes the group more able. Next we think about what is big when people are hurt by chance and cannot know what to do and they may get hurt. Some might say that staying away from the person who did the bad thing could keep the group safe. Even so the need for real learn and trust makes the person try to keep trying even when it feels like a hurt to the person who got it. So my answer is: The best way is for the person to let go of the idea that they must feel okay or not see what they do while they still work hard on their own work.
